questions.explanation: Each block includes the previous block’s hash; changing past data changes the hash, breaking the chain and revealing tampering.

options.A: To encrypt all transaction data so only miners can read it
options.B: To link blocks together and make tampering detectable
options.C: To generate private keys for users automatically
options.D: To compress the blockchain so it uses less storage

questions.question: In a blockchain, what is the primary purpose of a cryptographic hash in each block header?

questions.explanation: The EVM is the deterministic execution environment that runs contract bytecode and enforces gas rules across all nodes.

options.A: A database that stores only ERC-20 token balances
options.B: A peer-to-peer networking protocol for node discovery
options.C: A deterministic runtime that executes smart contract bytecode on Ethereum
options.D: A wallet standard for signing transactions

questions.question: Which statement best describes the Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM)?

questions.explanation: A `view` function promises not to modify state (no storage writes), allowing it to be called without sending a transaction.
questions.question: Which Solidity keyword is used to declare a function that does not modify blockchain state?

questions.explanation: ERC-721 defines NFTs with unique token IDs and ownership tracking per token.
questions.question: Which token standard is designed for non-fungible tokens (NFTs) where each token ID is unique?

questions.explanation: `eth_call` simulates execution against a node’s current state without writing to the chain; a transaction is mined and updates state.

options.A: `eth_call` changes state but costs no gas; transactions do not change state but cost gas
options.B: `eth_call` executes locally without persisting state changes; transactions execute on-chain and persist state changes
options.C: `eth_call` requires a mined block; transactions execute instantly
options.D: `eth_call` can only be used for payable functions; transactions cannot send ETH

questions.question: In Ethereum, what is the practical difference between calling a contract function with `eth_call` versus sending a transaction?

questions.explanation: Merkle trees allow compact proofs (Merkle proofs) that a transaction is included in a block using only a logarithmic number of hashes.

options.A: To hide transaction amounts using encryption
options.B: To enable efficient inclusion proofs for transactions without downloading the entire block
options.C: To generate validator identities for proof-of-stake
options.D: To prevent front-running by reordering transactions

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a Merkle tree in a blockchain block?

questions.explanation: Fuzz/property-based testing generates many randomized sequences/inputs to validate invariants hold under diverse conditions.

options.D: Manual testing on a public testnet

questions.question: Which testing approach is most appropriate for checking an invariant like “totalSupply always equals the sum of all balances” across many randomized inputs?

questions.explanation: Reentrancy is mitigated by updating state (effects) before external interactions, and often adding a reentrancy guard.

options.B: Use the Checks-Effects-Interactions pattern (update state before external calls)

questions.question: A contract sends ETH using `call` before updating internal balances. An attacker re-enters and drains funds. Which change most directly mitigates this vulnerability?

questions.explanation: Merkle airdrops keep only a root on-chain and shift per-user costs to claims, avoiding an expensive on-chain loop over thousands of recipients.

options.A: A single transaction that loops over all recipients and transfers to each
options.B: A pull-based claim system using a Merkle root (Merkle airdrop) where users claim individually
options.C: Storing the entire recipient list as an on-chain array and iterating over it per block
options.D: Deploying a separate ERC-20 contract per recipient

questions.question: You need to distribute tokens to 50,000 addresses. Which design is generally most gas-efficient and scalable on Ethereum mainnet?

questions.explanation: Public permissioned systems allow open read/verification while restricting who can write/submit transactions to approved participants.

options.B: Private permissioned blockchain with no public read access
options.C: Public permissioned (permissioned write, public read) blockchain
options.D: Offline blockchain with periodic manual synchronization

questions.question: You are building a dApp that must allow anyone to read and verify data, but only approved participants can submit transactions. Which blockchain openness model fits best?
